title: Ronald Kaoma Chitotela v Anti-Corruption Commission and 3 Ors (SCZ/8/1/2025) [2025] ZMSC 17 (13 August 2025)

paragraphs.text:
Thelma Maunga u. Anti-Corruption Commission and Attorney
General (Appeal No. 5 of2024)

JCNHoldings Ltd. v. Development Bank of Zambia& Others (SCZ
Judgment No. 22 of 2013)
Other work referred to:
Constitution of Zambia(Amendment) Act No. 2 of 2016

Forfeiture of ProceedsofCrime Act, No. 19 of 2010

High CourtAct Chapter 27 ofthe Laws of Zambia

Protection of FundamentalRights Regulations, 1969.
1.0. INTRODUCTION AND BACKGROUND
1.1. A question that is often asked in the anti-corruption efforts is
whether the end should justify the means? This and kindred
no
questions inevitably deserve consideration in this appeal,
matter how slight. More solemnly, the appeal is a stark
<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrr>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
J3
on
reminder the need for those involved in various roles in
investigating, prosecuting and trying corruption cases to always
-
temper their decisions with discernment good judgment,
SO aS
to avoid indicting innocence. Put simply, however the pursuit
of graft isdone, grafting injustice must be the last thing.
was a a
1.2. The appellant businessman and Member of Parliament
at the time these proceedings commenced in May 2022. He had
as a
previously served Cabinet Minister.
1.3. He is the alleged beneficial owner of the property known as
Stand No. CHONG/LN.21188/51 State Lodge, Chongwe,
though it is registered in the name ofa third party, Liu Runmin,
who issaid to have sold the property to the appellant.
an
1.4. In its investigations, the first respondent took interest in the
appellant's said property, suspecting it to have been acquired
using proceeds of crime. The fact that the property was still
name
registered in the of Liu Runmin only heightened the first
respondent's suspicion. The appellant explains that the only
reason the property is not yet registered in his name is because
<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrr>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
J4
some largely administrative aspects of the sale transaction with
Liu Runmin have not been completed.
1.5. The first respondent issued a restriction notice pursuant to the
over
provisions of the Anti-Corruption Commission Act the
property on 22nd March 2022. Through his advocates the
appellant challenged the restriction notice in the High Court.
an
1.6. Subsequently, the first respondent invited the appellant for
interview on the 20th April 2022 whereupon he was warned and
cautioned on a possible charge of being in possession of
property reasonably suspected to be proceeds of crime.
1.7. On 29th April 2022, a seizure notice in respect of the appellant's
soon
property was served on the appellant's advOcates, and
thereafter the first respondent announced the seizure of the
property to the public.
2.0. A PETITION ISFILED
2.1. The appellant was angered by these developments as he had
maintained through-out that he purchased a bare plot of land
was constructed. He alleges that he
on which a dwelling house
<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrr>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
J5
used a construction company to put up the development and
had not even completed paying the bill for the construction
works at the time the property was seized by the first
respondent. He believed that by its actions, the first respondent
had violated several ofhis fundamental rights guaranteed under
the Constitution.
2.2. The appellant thus filed a petition in the High Court, General
Division, on 26th May 2023, pursuant to Article 28 of the
Constitution which provides that any person who believes that
his rights have been, are being or are likely to be infringed, may
petition the High Court for redress.
2.3. In his petition, the appellant alleged various breaches by the
first respondent of his fundamental rights as set out in the
Constitution. He claimed that the first respondent's entry upon
his property and the purported seizure of the same without his
consent violated, among others, his right to privacy under
Article 17(1)of the Constitution of Zambia.
2.4. The appellant also alleged violation of his right to a fair trial
under Article 18 of the Constitution in that the seizure by the
<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrr>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
J6
first respondent of his dwelling house meant that his right to be
was
presumed innocent had been violated. He posited that it
incumbent upon the first respondent to prove the appellant
guilty before any seizure ofhis property. He also contended that
he could not be compelled to give evidence; that the first
respondent seized his property without ascertaining where the
resources he used to purchase and construct the same came
from.
2.5. He sought a series of relief including a declaration that his
property was legally and genuinely acquired; and that the
purported seizure of his property was arbitrary and in
contravention of his rights under the Constitution.
2.6. The appellant challenged sections 58 and 59 of the Anti
Corruption Commission Act, alleging that they are
unconstitutional as they deprive him of his right to privacy, to
his property and to due process of the law.
2.7. More deliberately,the appellant alleged that sections 71 and 78
are unconstitutional
of the Forfeiture of Proceeds of Crime Act
as they contravene Article 18(2)(a) and (7) of the Constitution.
<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrr>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
J7
2.8. Before the hearing of the petition, several preliminary issues
Were raised. One of them impugned the jurisdiction of the
Economic and Financial Crimes Division (EFCC) to hear a
human rights matter.
on
2.9. In his motion the jurisdictional question before the EFCC,
the appellant pointed to section 3 ofthe Economic and Financial
Crimes (Division of Court) Order 2022 in supporting his
submission that the court was created to hear and determine
cases pertaining to economic and financial crimes and
corruption and not those alleging violations of fundamental
rights under theBill of Rights and as such he would be severely
prejudiced if that court heard and determined his grievances
under the Bill of Rights.
2.10. He also raised concern that the file's movement from the
was not commnunicated to the
General Division to the EFCC
parties.
court gave short shrift to the objection, holding that the
2.11. The
EFCC was mnerely constit

Ronald Kaoma Chitotela v. Anti-Corruption Commission &s
Others Cause No. 2022/HP/0773.

Referral of Cause No. 2022/HP/0660 Ronald Kaoma

Trywell Katatula Kashawa & The Attorney General
2015/HP/1931.
7.26. Counsel contended that it is clear from this correspondence
exchanged that there was no order as required by section 23 in
case case
transferring the files. Citing the of JCN Holdings Ltd.
our
v. Development Bank of Zambia & Others(2), counsel rehashed
case reasons
holding in that that there should be necessitating
any cases case,
transfer of between judges. In this there
appears to be a directive premised on a characterisation of the
case.
<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrr>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
J25
7.27. What is clear to us is that this was not an ordinary transfer of
a case record from one judge to another as envisaged in section
23 of the High Court Act. It was a surrender of a file from the
handlingjudge in the General Division
tothe Judge-in-Charge,
case
General Division, and the subseguent transfer of the
record from the GeneralDivision to the EFCC.
cases to iudges of the High Court 1s
7.28. The allocation of
Judge-in-Charge. Once a judge
administratively done by the
has taken conduct of a case, they are expected to conclude it.
any reason, continueto deal
Where, however they cannot, for
with the case and it becomes necessary that thehandling judge
passes the file to another judge, section 23 of the High Court
an
Act becomes applicable. The handling judge must make
order (in writing)and must secure the consent of the transferee
judge.
7.29. We note that section 23 of the Act does not envisage the return
of a file to the Judge-in-Charge for purposes ofreallocation. The
reason for this is not far to seek. The Act does not provide for
<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrr>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
J26
the administrative position of Judge-in-Charge and the role
he/she plays in allocating files.
7.30. "There is, of course, a difference between a transfer and an
allocation. An allocation is clearly not a transfer. However, a
reallocation of a case could well be a transfer from the initial
judge toanother judge save that chain is broken at the point of
the Judge-in-Charge's administrative intervention.
7.31.Although returning a case record to the Judge-in-Charge is with
a clear understanding by both the judge returning the file and
the Judge-in-Charge that the file is to be reallocated to another
judge, we believe that such return tothe Judge-in-Charge being
a transfer of a file, still requires compliance with section 23.
There ought to be an order by the transferringjudge on the file.
At a more practical level, however, the consent of the Judge-in
Charge is not necessary since he is not the judge that wil1
handle the matter.
7.32. In the case of Thelma Maunga v. Attorney General(1) we held that:
<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrrr>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
J27
the matter was not transferred in accordance with the law
as
laid down in section 23 ofthe High Court Act. The transfer
was irregular. The implication was that the court that heard
the petition had no jurisdiction to hear it.
7.33. In the present case, from the correspondence exchanged
between the judge initially given conduct of this matter and the
Judge-in-Charge, the reason for the transfer of the case file to
case as an
the EFCC was the mischaracterisation of the
was not.
economic and financial crimes matter when it clearly
There is no evidence that an order was made in transferring the
file to the Judge-in-Charge (General Division) or indeed tothe
judges of the EFCC, nor is there anything on record confirming
the consent of the transferee judges.
7.34. As in Thelma Maunga(), we hold that the transfer or allocation of
the case to the EFCC was irregular. In the result, the EFCC
consequence,
lacked jurisdiction to hear the matter. In the
judgment of the court is hereby squashed.
7.35, We order that thematter be remitted to the High Court to be
cause
heard in the General Division under the original number.
<<<<<<<<<<bbbbbbbbbbrrrrrrrrrr>>>>>>>>>>
J28
7.36. As the irregularity was occasioned by the court's own
mischaracterisation of the matter, we order that each party
bears its own costs.
Mumba Malila
CHIEF JUSTICE
F.M. Chisanga
R. M. C. Kaoma
SUPREME COURT JUDGE
SUPREME COURT JUDGE
